title: An Optimized Franz-Parisi Criterion and its Equivalence with SQ Lower Bounds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a generalized Franz–Parisi (GFP) hardness criterion for statistical detection that optimizes the “overlap” event under a mild group-invariance constraint, and proves an equivalence between this GFP criterion and Statistical Query (SQ) lower bounds under a natural correlation-type assumption (Assumption 1). The authors show that Assumption 1 is satisfied for a wide range of models (Gaussian additive models, planted sparse models, NGCA, single-index models, convex truncation), thereby unifying and simplifying several known SQ lower bounds and yielding new ones (e.g., mixed sparse linear regression and convex truncation). The work also identifies settings where the original FP criterion fails to capture hardness while the GFP criterion succeeds.
strengths: Introduces a principled optimization of the FP overlap set via group-invariant events and a data-driven overlap function ρ_G(u, v), addressing known mismatches of the Euclidean overlap with model geometry.
Establishes a two-way equivalence between GFP-hardness and SQ-hardness (up to natural slack factors) under Assumption 1, extending previous FP↔LD connections beyond Gaussian additive models.
Connects GFP equivalence to low-degree (LD) bounds for noise-robust models via SQ↔LD results, substantially broadening the scope of FP-style predictions.
Provides a clean structural simplification (Theorem 2) that reduces GFP to a tractable ρ_G-based form, making the criterion easier to apply.
While theoretical, the paper validates applicability through a diverse suite of examples: GAMs (with symmetric priors), planted sparse models, NGCA, single-index models, and convex truncation.
Gives new SQ lower bounds (e.g., mixed sparse linear regression; convex truncation) and recovers prior ones via a single unifying argument.
Presents a counterexample where FP-hardness and GFP-hardness diverge, demonstrating the necessity of the optimization and the inappropriateness of Euclidean overlap in some models.
Provides precise formulations of GFP, ρ_G-FP hardness, and SQ hardness, and states the main theorems cleanly with explicit conditions and parameter trade-offs.
Offers intuition for Assumption 1 (group-averaged nonnegativity of centered inner-product moments) and explains why small finite groups (trivial or Z2) suffice in key examples.
Advances the conceptual bridge between physics-inspired hardness criteria and rigorous algorithmic lower bounds, now encompassing SQ alongside LD.
Unifies disparate lower-bound techniques, reducing the burden of case-by-case SQ analyses in several canonical detection problems.
Strengthens the theoretical understanding of when the FP potential correctly predicts hardness and when a geometry-aware generalization is needed.
weaknesses: The equivalence results hinge on Assumption 1; although mild in many models, it is nontrivial, and the paper would benefit from tighter characterizations of when it fails and what that implies.
Some parts of the equivalence require technical conditions (e.g., parity of m, existence of r with exact mass 1 − q^−2), which are argued to be removable but currently complicate the main statements.
The reliance on the annealed potential persists; the relationship to the quenched FP potential remains open, leaving a gap with the original physics intuition.

The mapping between query complexity q, degree D, and concrete polynomial-time runtimes is discussed at a high level; a more explicit operational interpretation (especially for non-noise-robust models) would aid practitioners.

Some new SQ lower bounds (e.g., convex truncation) are stated at a conceptual level; more explicit threshold statements and tightness discussions relative to concrete algorithms could strengthen the contribution.
Assumption 1, while intuitive post-hoc in examples, could use more front-loaded intuition, equivalent reformulations, or geometric interpretations beyond the Z2/trivial cases.
Several technical slack terms (e.g., dependence on |G|, χ² factors at sample scale 4t) are scattered; consolidating them into a single “equivalence loss” summary would improve readability.
Minor citation alignment: some attributions (e.g., NGCA SQ lower bounds) could be clarified to ensure precise matching to existing literature.
The discussion about when FP and GFP differ could be broadened with additional counterexamples or a taxonomy of models where Euclidean overlap is provably suboptimal.
More explicit comparison with recent extensions of LD to estimation tasks would help position this work relative to that emerging line.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide additional equivalent characterizations or sufficient conditions for Assumption 1 beyond GCI and Z2 symmetry cases, especially for models with richer group actions (e.g., rotations, permutations)?
2. In Theorem 3 (reverse direction), how tight are the dependencies on t, |G|, and χ²(P⊗4t||Q⊗4t)? Are there settings where these terms can be systematically improved, or is there a lower bound showing they are inherent?
3. For the convex truncation SQ lower bound, could you state an explicit sample-complexity threshold and demonstrate tightness with the SODA’23 algorithm side by side, including constants where feasible?
4. The Section 5 counterexample is compelling; can you generalize criteria for when the Euclidean FP criterion is expected to fail, perhaps giving a checklist (e.g., non-spherical likelihood geometry or likelihood inner products not being functions of dot products)?
5. Could you discuss how one might algorithmically exploit ρ_G (or its level sets) for designing practical tests, especially in models where FP and GFP diverge? Are there tractable estimators for ρ_G in common families?
6. How sensitive are your equivalence statements to moving from annealed to quenched FP potentials? Are there natural counterexamples where annealed GFP and quenched FP diverge in predicted hardness?
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a substantial and timely contribution by generalizing the Franz–Parisi hardness criterion to a group-invariant, geometry-aware form and proving its equivalence to Statistical Query hardness under a mild and widely satisfied assumption. It both unifies prior lower-bound techniques and yields new insights and results in important models, while also clarifying when the original FP criterion can be misleading. The technical development is careful and broadly applicable; the examples convincingly demonstrate scope. Some aspects could be tightened—particularly the explicit statement of new SQ thresholds, more intuition and characterizations for Assumption 1, and a crisper alignment with certain related works—but these are refinements rather than fundamental issues. Overall, this work will be valuable to the NeurIPS community working on computational-statistical tradeoffs, and I recommend acceptance.
